varieties.name: RIVERSIDE
varieties.origin: Australia selection for subtropical regions
varieties.traits: Medium-large oval nuts, thin shell, precocious bearer, upright growth habit
varieties.yield_notes: Heavy cropper in subtropical areas, suitable for high-density plantings

varieties.name: MOHAWK
varieties.origin: USDA Pecan Breeding, released 1946, Named after Mohawk Nation
varieties.traits: Very large nuts (45-50/lb), soft shell, moderate vigor
varieties.yield_notes: Heavy early production, young trees very productive
varieties.growing_notes: Suitable for Brisbane with irrigation. Low chill suitable for subtropics. Performance declines in older trees. Popular in QLD commercial plantings. Type A pollinator. Pairs with Western Schley.
varieties.availability: Australian pecan nurseries, common commercial variety

varieties.name: PAWNEE
varieties.origin: USDA Brownwood TX 1963 (Mohawk x Starking Hardy Giant), released 1984
varieties.traits: Large nuts (51-58/lb), thin shell, upright growth, attractive appearance
varieties.yield_notes: Good yields, moderate alternate bearing, precocious (bears 5-7 years)

varieties.name: STUART
varieties.origin: Mississippi 1874, introduced 1886 - one of oldest named varieties
varieties.traits: Large nuts (50/lb), medium-thick shell, strong upright tree
varieties.yield_notes: Heavy consistent bearer, slower to mature (8-10 years)

varieties.name: DESIRABLE
varieties.origin: Mississippi early 1900s (likely Success x Jewett), released 1945
varieties.traits: Very large nuts (39-48/lb), soft medium shell, easy to crack
varieties.yield_notes: Consistent heavy production, reliable annual bearer

varieties.name: WICHITA
varieties.origin: USDA Brownwood TX 1940 (Halbert x Mahan), released 1959
varieties.traits: Large nuts (50-62/lb), moderately upright, vigorous, purplish-black stripes on shell
varieties.yield_notes: Very prolific, precocious (bears early 5-6 years), tendency to overbear
varieties.growing_notes: Suitable for cooler subtropics/warm temperate Brisbane area. Type A pollinator - excellent for Western Schley. Light producer early, heavy after year 12. Thick shell. Prefers dry climate but adapts with management.
varieties.availability: Daley's Fruit Trees QLD, Australian pecan nurseries

varieties.name: WESTERN SCHLEY
varieties.origin: San Saba TX seedling discovery 1895, developed by E.E. Risien
varieties.traits: Small oblong nuts (72-77/lb), thin smooth shell, productive
varieties.yield_notes: Very productive in dry climates, self-pollinator, consistent bearer

varieties.name: BURKETT
varieties.origin: Callahan County TX native seedling, introduced 1911
varieties.traits: Large round nuts (uniformly round shape), thin shell, natural symmetry
varieties.yield_notes: Moderate, late ripening variety

varieties.name: CADDO
varieties.origin: USDA Georgia 1922-23 (Brooks x Alley), released 1968
varieties.traits: Medium-small elongated nuts (67-70/lb), football shaped, slender
varieties.yield_notes: One of best yielders - highly productive, consistent annual bearer, precocious
varieties.growing_notes: Excellent for Brisbane subtropical climate. Strong limbs, disease resistant foliage (dark green). Early-mid ripening (October 11). Moderate scab resistance - manageable with standard spray. Bears 5-6 years. Type I pollinator. Watch for black aphids. Outstanding reliable producer.

varieties.name: ELLIOTT
varieties.origin: Florida seedling 1912, discovered by Henry Elliot
varieties.traits: Small teardrop-shaped nuts (62+/lb), smooth symmetrical, distinctive shape
varieties.yield_notes: Consistent producer, alternate bearing pattern, quality maintained in heavy years
varieties.growing_notes: SPECIALTY PECAN - premium pricing. EXCELLENT scab resistance - ideal for Brisbane humid climate. Drought tolerant, disease resistant. Type II pollinator. Pairs with Pawnee, Caddo, Kanza. Sought after by candy makers, chefs. High oil content exceptional flavor. Slow to produce initially but very reliable. Perfect for subtropical Florida/Brisbane conditions.
varieties.availability: Australian nurseries, highly recommended for Brisbane

varieties.name: KANZA
varieties.origin: USDA 1996 (Major x Shoshoni), named after Kanza Nation of Kansas
varieties.traits: Small round nuts (65-74/lb), attractive, similar appearance to Elliott
varieties.yield_notes: Good mature production, some alternate bearing

varieties.name: CHEYENNE
varieties.origin: USDA 1970 (Clark x Odom), named after Cheyenne Nation
varieties.traits: Medium nuts (55-60/lb), distinctive wrinkled kernel appearance, small tree with lateral branching
varieties.yield_notes: Highly productive, precocious (5-7 years), severe alternate bearing tendency

varieties.name: MAHAN
varieties.origin: 1927 variety, named after J.C. Mahan
varieties.traits: VERY LARGE nuts (40-50/lb), soft shell, vigorous tree
varieties.yield_notes: Heavy early bearer, production declines with age
varieties.growing_notes: HEIRLOOM variety (100 years). Eye appeal from large size but kernel filling issues. Prefers arid warm winter climates. Mid-late November ripening. Used extensively in breeding programs (parent of many varieties). Type A pollinator. NOT recommended for new plantings - superseded by better varieties. Historical significance only.

varieties.name: CAPE FEAR
varieties.origin: North Carolina 1937 (Schley seedling)
varieties.traits: Medium nuts (55-60/lb), thin shell, resembles Stuart in size/shape
varieties.yield_notes: Very precocious, early strong bearer, can overload

varieties.name: SUMNER
varieties.origin: USDA breeding program
varieties.traits: Medium nuts, elongated shape, thin shell, attractive visual appeal
varieties.yield_notes: Good producer, consistent
varieties.growing_notes: GOOD scab resistance - recommended for Brisbane humid climate. Disease resistant, easy-to-peel nuts. Great shade tree. Type II pollinator. Pairs with Cape Fear, Creek. Late-season pollinizer. Water needs when young. Well-suited subtropical/commercial use.

varieties.name: CHOCTAW
varieties.origin: USDA 1950s-70s release, named after Choctaw Nation
varieties.traits: Large nuts, thin hull, resembles Western Schley appearance
varieties.yield_notes: Moderate, requires perfect management

varieties.name: KIOWA
varieties.origin: USDA 1976 (Mahan x Odom parentage, likely Mahan x Desirable)
varieties.traits: VERY LARGE nuts (45/lb), high-percentage kernel
varieties.yield_notes: High yield potential, alternate bearing tendency
varieties.growing_notes: Late season maturity (late October-early November). Commonly recommended for Southeast U.S. Great scab resistance. Type A pollinator. Large size impressive but requires long growing season - suitable for Brisbane if season length adequate. Commercial favorite for size/quality combination.

varieties.name: OCONEE
varieties.origin: University of Georgia 1989 release
varieties.traits: Large nuts, cold-resistant, low-maintenance hybrid
varieties.yield_notes: Good, consistent

varieties.name: SIOUX
varieties.origin: USDA (Schley x Carmichael cross)
varieties.traits: Small nuts, thin shell, very light color, gorgeous tree form
varieties.yield_notes: Good
varieties.growing_notes: ONE OF BEST EATING QUALITY - top flavor pecan. Small size excellent for mixed nut applications. Requires fungicide in HIGH HUMIDITY areas - Brisbane would need sprays. Little training required - recommended best homeowner tree. Type A pollinator. Fresh flavor best in world. Native pecan flavor quality without hard shell.

varieties.name: NATIVE/WILD PECANS
varieties.origin: Indigenous North America - discovered 16th century by European explorers, staple food for Native Americans since ancient times
varieties.traits: Variable size/shape (70-150/lb typical), thick hard shells, natural genetic diversity tree to tree
varieties.yield_notes: Variable, no irrigation typically, rely on natural rainfall
varieties.growing_notes: INDIGENOUS HEIRLOOM - oldest pecan type. Algonquin word 'pacane' = 'nut so hard requires stone to crack'. EXCELLENT disease resistance due to genetic variability. Heat-tolerant (especially Texas natives). Sustainable - no irrigation/fertilizer/spraying. Best for pecan pieces in candies/pralines/pies. Shell thickness natural protection from animals. Flavor superior to clones - stress improves taste. NOT suitable Brisbane (requires sourcing from wild North American trees).
varieties.availability: NOT available Australia - U.S. wild harvest only (Texas, Missouri)
